It is common to see platforms that fail to be adopted, are incompatible with established processes, or simply “digitalize paper” without simplifying the underlying tasks. Large-scale technology projects often fall into a recurring pattern: [cost overruns, delays, and lower-than-expected benefits.](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2742088)

excerpts: In this paper, we conduct the first empirical study of how United States cities' procurement practices shape critical decisions surrounding public sector AI.
citations.title: Legacy Procurement Practices Shape How U.S. Cities Govern AI

executive_summary.description: A high-level overview of the documented failure modes, adoption barriers, and equity gaps for civic technology solutions in government procurement, summarizing the key challenges and opportunities.

key_failure_modes.description: Detailed analysis of the common failure modes for procurement automation tools, including technical shortcomings, projects being treated as 'vanity projects', and a mismatch between the solution and the existing governmental challenges.

staff_adoption_barriers.description: An examination of the human-centric barriers to adopting new procurement technologies, such as resistance to change, fear of job displacement, lack of digital skills, insufficient training, and challenges in change management.

organizational_and_systemic_barriers.description: Discussion of systemic challenges within government that hinder technology adoption, including outdated legacy IT infrastructure, siloed procurement offices, lack of interoperability standards, and convoluted procurement processes for acquiring new technology.

benchmark_dataset.description: The name of the benchmark dataset used for evaluation, such as the Contract Understanding Atticus Dataset (CUAD).

model_type.description: The type of AI model evaluated (e.g., BERT-based models).

performance_metric.description: The specific performance metric reported in research (e.g., 'Precision @ 80% Recall', F1-score).

accuracy_score.description: The reported accuracy or performance score from the research findings.

limitations.description: Noted limitations or challenges in AI-based data extraction from legal documents.

risk_description.description: A description of the risk, primarily vendor lock-in where a government becomes dependent on a specific provider.

causes.description: The root causes of vendor lock-in, such as proprietary technologies, lack of open standards, and cooperative purchasing agreements.

impact_on_cities.description: The negative consequences for municipalities, including reduced leverage in negotiations and difficulty switching vendors.

mitigation_strategies.description: Strategies to avoid or reduce vendor lock-in, such as opting for modular system design and enforcing open data standards.

identified_barriers.description: Specific barriers faced by MBE/SWaM vendors, such as complex paper-based processes, opaque systems, and difficulty learning about opportunities.

impact_of_automation.description: How automation can either exacerbate these barriers (if designed poorly) or alleviate them (if designed with user needs in mind).

key_recommendations.description: Solutions to improve equity, such as prompt payment, user-centered design research with vendors, and simplifying requirements.

standard_name.description: The name of the data standard, primarily the Open Contracting Data Standard (OCDS).

purpose.description: The goal of the standard, which is to make government contracting data open, accessible, and usable for oversight and analysis.

key_data_structures.description: Core components of the standard, such as the Release Package, which contains metadata and a list of releases for a contracting process.

relevance_to_automation.description: How adopting the standard provides a foundation for building transparent and interoperable procurement automation tools.

city_or_region.description: The city, state, or country where the implementation took place.

system_name.description: The name of the procurement system or tool (e.g., Ukraine's Prozorro).

positive_outcomes.description: The documented positive results, such as significant cost savings, increased competition, and improved public transparency.

key_success_factors.description: The factors that contributed to success, such as building on the Open Contracting Data Standard and developing automated risk indicators for auditors.

successful_implementation_case_studies.description: Examples of cities or governments where procurement automation or AI-powered contract analysis tools have succeeded. Each case study will detail the solution implemented (e.g., Ukraine's Prozorro), the positive outcomes (e.g., cost savings, increased competition), and key success factors.

city_or_region.description: The city or region experiencing challenges with its procurement system.

system_name.description: The name of the procurement system (e.g., NYC PASSPort, LA RAMP).

documented_problems.description: Specific issues reported, such as significant contract and payment delays, user complaints from vendors, and failure to centralize opportunities effectively.

analysis_of_failure.description: The underlying reasons for the problems, such as systemic complexity, process bottlenecks, and a failure to address user needs.

failed_or_problematic_implementation_cas.description: Examples of cities where procurement automation has failed or faced significant challenges. Each case study will detail the system (e.g., NYC PASSPort, LA RAMP), the documented problems (e.g., delays, user complaints, failure to meet goals), and the reasons for the failure.

audit_type.description: The type of audit where AI is applied, particularly ex-ante (preventive) audits that occur before a contract is finalized.

ai_function.description: The specific function the AI performs, such as flagging non-compliant clauses, detecting anomalies in pricing, and identifying fraud red flags.

example_risk_indicators.description: Examples of risks or red flags AI can identify, such as disqualifying all but one bidder or unusual payment schedules.

benefit.description: The primary advantage of using AI, which is enabling real-time monitoring and shifting from reactive to proactive, preventive oversight.

emerging_governance_practices_for_ai_pro.description: An overview of new actions and strategies cities are taking to govern the procurement of AI, such as modifying RFP templates with AI-specific questions, adding new contract language for algorithmic systems, and establishing separate AI review processes.

technical_prerequisites_for_ai_adoption.description: A summary of the foundational technical requirements for successfully implementing AI in procurement, including the complete digitization of contracts (using OCR), centralized data management, and establishing direct, real-time data access for audit and oversight bodies.

recommendations_for_equitable_automation.description: Actionable recommendations for government agencies to implement procurement automation in a way that is effective, equitable, and avoids common pitfalls. Includes strategies for user-centered design, improving access for MBE/SWaM vendors, and ensuring accountability.
